section.title: Feeding the Five Thousand and Walking on Water
section.sectionRationale: Grieved by John's death, Jesus withdraws, yet has compassion on the crowds and feeds five thousand from five loaves and two fish, the only miracle in all four Gospels. That same night He walks to His disciples on the storm-tossed sea, rescues sinking Peter, and stills the wind, drawing from the boat the first full confession: truly You are the Son of God.
section.thesis: The compassionate Shepherd who feeds the multitude in the wilderness from almost nothing is the divine Lord who walks upon the sea and speaks the name of God, so that His provision and His mastery over creation together compel the worship of His disciples: truly You are the Son of God.
section.frameNote: Three beats: compassion and the feeding of the five thousand; Jesus walking on the water and rescuing Peter, leading to worship; and the healings at Gennesaret.

units.label: Compassion and the feeding
units.note: five loaves, two fish, twelve baskets

verses.text: When Jesus heard about John, He withdrew by boat privately to a solitary place. But the crowds found out about it and followed Him on foot from the towns.
When He stepped ashore and saw a large crowd, He had {{splanchnizomai|compassion}} on them and healed their sick.
When evening came, the disciples came to Him and said, “This is a desolate place, and the hour is already late. Dismiss the crowds so they can go to the villages and buy themselves some food.”
“They do not need to go away,” Jesus replied. “You give them something to eat.”
“We have here only five loaves of bread and two fish,” they answered.
“Bring them here to Me,” Jesus said.

And He directed the crowds to sit down on the grass. Taking the five loaves and the two fish and looking up to heaven, He spoke a {{eulogeo|blessing}}. Then He broke the loaves and gave them to the disciples, and the disciples gave them to the people.
They all ate and were satisfied, and the disciples picked up twelve basketfuls of broken pieces that were left over.
About five thousand men were fed, besides women and children.

units.label: Walking on the water
units.note: truly You are the Son of God

verses.text: Immediately Jesus made the disciples get into the boat and go on ahead of Him to the other side, while He dismissed the crowds.
After He had dismissed them, He went up on the mountain by Himself to pray. When evening came, He was there alone,
but the boat was already far from land, buffeted by the waves because the wind was against it.
During the fourth watch of the night, Jesus went out to them, walking on the sea.
When the disciples saw Him walking on the sea, they were terrified. “It’s a ghost!” they said, and cried out in fear.
But Jesus spoke up at once: “Take courage! {{egoeimi|It is I}}. Do not be afraid.”
“Lord, if it is You,” Peter replied, “command me to come to You on the water.”
“Come,” said Jesus. Then Peter got down out of the boat, walked on the water, and came toward Jesus.
But when he saw the strength of the wind, he was afraid and, beginning to sink, cried out, “Lord, save me!”
Immediately Jesus reached out His hand and took hold of Peter. “You of little faith,” He said, “why did you doubt?”
And when they had climbed back into the boat, the wind died down.
Then those who were in the boat {{proskyneo|worshiped}} Him, saying, “Truly You are the Son of God!”

units.label: Healing at Gennesaret
units.note: all who touched Him were healed

verses.text: When they had crossed over, they landed at Gennesaret.
And when the men of that place recognized Jesus, they sent word to all the surrounding region. People brought all the sick to Him
and begged Him just to let them touch the fringe of His cloak. And all who touched Him were healed.

splanchnizomai.gloss: To be moved with compassion in one's inmost being. Though grieving John's death and seeking solitude, Jesus is moved to His core by the crowd's need, and heals and feeds them; His mercy overflows even His own sorrow.

eulogeo.gloss: To bless, give thanks, speak well of. Jesus looks to heaven and blesses the loaves before breaking and distributing them; the gestures, take, bless, break, give, echo the Passover and anticipate the Last Supper.

egoeimi.gloss: It is I, literally I am. On the sea Jesus' reassurance carries the resonance of the divine name revealed to Moses, Exodus 3:14; the One who walks the waves speaks with the voice of God, calming fear.

proskyneo.gloss: To worship, fall down before, do homage. After the storm the disciples worship Jesus and confess Him the Son of God, the response due to God alone, drawn out by His mastery over the sea.

basic.context: Hearing of John's death, Jesus withdraws to be alone, but the crowds follow, and He has compassion, healing and then feeding them. From five loaves and two fish He satisfies five thousand men plus women and children, with twelve baskets left over. That night He sends the disciples ahead, prays alone, and then comes to them walking on the storm-tossed sea. He rescues Peter, who tried and began to sink, stills the wind, and the disciples worship Him as the Son of God.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the feeding as more than a miracle of provision: the wilderness setting, the shepherd's compassion, and the take-bless-break-give pattern recall the manna, the Shepherd of Psalm 23, and point toward the Lord's Supper. Read the sea-walking as a revelation of Jesus' deity: only God treads the waves, and His words carry the divine I am. Together they answer who Jesus is.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus is moved with compassion, a gut-deep mercy, even in His own grief. He blesses the loaves with the gestures that echo the Passover and the Supper to come. On the sea His words, It is I, carry the sound of the divine name, I am. And the disciples worship Him, giving Him the homage due to God, confessing Him the Son of God.
basic.typology: Jesus feeding the crowd in the wilderness fulfills the pattern of God feeding Israel with manna and pictures the Messiah's banquet. As the Shepherd of Psalm 23, He makes them lie down in green pastures. And His walking on the sea takes up what Scripture says of God alone, who treads the waves and stills the storm.
basic.christ: Jesus is the compassionate Shepherd who provides abundantly from almost nothing, the true bread who satisfies. And He is the divine Lord who walks upon the sea and speaks the name of God, mastering the chaos that terrifies His disciples. Their confession is the point: truly You are the Son of God.
basic.god: The God who fed Israel in the wilderness and who alone rules the sea is present and at work in Jesus. His compassion meets human need with overflowing abundance, and His power over creation is absolute. He is worthy of worship, and He reaches out to rescue the sinking who cry to Him.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 16 gives the manna in the wilderness. Psalm 23 is the Shepherd who provides. Job 9 says God alone treads on the waves of the sea. And Matthew 26 echoes the take, bless, break, give of the Last Supper.
basic.oneStory: In the wake of rejection and grief, Jesus reveals His glory more brightly than ever. He is the Shepherd who feeds the hungry multitude in the wilderness and the divine Lord who walks the raging sea and rescues the sinking. The disciples fall down in worship with the confession the whole Gospel has been building toward: truly You are the Son of God, the King who provides and saves.

context.literary: The feeding and the sea-walking form a linked pair, both set at evening around the Sea of Galilee, and they answer the question of Jesus' identity raised by the parables and the rejection. The disciples' worship and confession in verse 33 mark a high point in Matthew, anticipating Peter's confession in chapter 16. The healings at Gennesaret round off the section.
context.historical: The feeding is the only miracle recorded in all four Gospels, underscoring its importance. Five thousand men, besides women and children, implies a crowd of many thousands. The fourth watch of the night was between three and six in the morning. The Sea of Galilee's sudden storms and the disciples' fishing background make their terror at a figure on the water vivid.
context.canonical: The wilderness feeding evokes the manna of Exodus 16 and the shepherd imagery of Psalm 23 and Ezekiel 34, presenting Jesus as the messianic Shepherd. The walking on the sea and the words It is I draw on texts where God alone masters the waters, Job 9:8 and Psalm 77:19, and the divine self-revelation of Exodus 3:14, so the disciples' confession of Jesus as Son of God is the fitting response.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal Jesus as the compassionate messianic Shepherd who provides abundantly and as the divine Lord who masters the sea, so that His provision and His power together draw out the worshipful confession that He is the Son of God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The miracles describe Jesus' compassion and deity. Peter's venture and rescue teach lessons about faith and doubt and about crying to Christ when sinking, and the disciples' worship models the fitting response. The passage does not promise identical miracles but reveals who Jesus is and calls for trust and worship.

debates.question: What does Jesus walking on the sea and saying It is I reveal about Him?

views.label: A disclosure of His deity
views.case: Scripture says God alone treads the waves and stills the storm; Jesus doing so, and speaking the words It is I that echo the divine name, unveils Him as God present with His people, drawing the confession Son of God.

views.label: A rescue that reveals His lordship over chaos
views.case: The scene shows Jesus as Lord over the sea, the ancient emblem of chaos and death, coming to His storm-tossed disciples to save them; His mastery over the deep marks His divine authority.

debates.bearing: Both hold that the sea-walking is a theophany, a showing of God in Jesus, so that worship and the confession of His deity are the only fitting response.

debates.question: What does Peter's walking and sinking teach?

views.label: Faith fixed on Christ, endangered by fear
views.case: Peter walks while looking to Jesus and begins to sink when he sees the wind; the lesson is that faith fastened on Christ can do the impossible, while fear and doubt make us sink.

views.label: The rescue of the sinking who cry out
views.case: The heart of the scene is that when Peter cries, Lord, save me, Jesus at once reaches out and holds him; even little faith that calls on Christ is not abandoned but caught by His hand.

debates.bearing: Both teach that we must keep our eyes on Christ amid the storm, and that when we falter and cry to Him, He is quick to save, gently rebuking our doubt while holding us fast.

study.originalLanguages: Even in grief and seeking solitude, Jesus is splanchnizomai, moved with gut-deep compassion, and He heals and feeds the crowd. Taking the loaves, He eulogeo, blesses, with the gestures, take, bless, break, give, that echo the Passover and foreshadow the Supper. On the water His reassurance, ego eimi, It is I, literally I am, carries the resonance of the divine name revealed to Moses, so that the One walking the waves speaks with the voice of God. And when the storm is stilled, the disciples proskyneo, worship Him, giving Him the homage due to God alone and confessing Him truly the Son of God.

typology.fulfillment: As God fed Israel with manna, Jesus feeds the multitude in a desolate place and is Himself the true bread of life, Exodus 16 and John 6:35.
Jesus makes the crowd sit on the grass and satisfies them, the Shepherd of Psalm 23 who leads to green pastures and a full table.

typology.type: The Lord who treads the sea
typology.fulfillment: What Scripture ascribes to God alone, walking on the waves and stilling the storm, Jesus does, revealing His deity, Job 9:8 and Psalm 77:19.

study.christ: These twin miracles unveil Christ in His glory. He is the compassionate Shepherd who, even while grieving, cannot turn away from the crowds' need, and who feeds thousands to satisfaction from a boy's lunch, with abundance to spare. And He is the divine Lord who comes to His frightened disciples walking upon the sea, speaking the very name of God, It is I, and rescuing sinking Peter with an outstretched hand. The take, bless, break, and give of the loaves points ahead to the bread He will break at the Last Supper and to His body given for us. And the storm-tossed disciples, seeing His power over creation, fall down and confess what the whole Gospel proclaims: truly You are the Son of God.
study.god: The God who fed His people in the wilderness and who alone rules the raging sea is unmistakably present in Jesus. His compassion overflows even His own sorrow, meeting human hunger with lavish abundance, and His power over wind and wave is absolute, for the deep that terrifies us is under His feet. He does not stand aloof from His struggling people but comes to them across the storm, and when they cry out in their sinking, He reaches down to save. Such a God is worthy of the worship the disciples give, and He welcomes the trembling faith that calls on Him for rescue.

crossReferences.note: God feeds Israel with manna in the wilderness, the background to the feeding of the five thousand.
The Shepherd who makes His people lie down in green pastures and provides for them.
God alone treads on the waves of the sea, the act Jesus performs, revealing His deity.
Jesus took bread, blessed, broke, and gave it, the same pattern as the feeding, pointing to the Lord's Supper.

study.oneStory: After rejection and grief, the glory of Christ shines out. He is the Shepherd who feeds the hungry multitude in the wilderness from almost nothing, and the divine Lord who walks the storm-tossed sea, speaks the name of God, and lifts sinking Peter by the hand. The disciples fall down in worship with the confession the whole story has been reaching toward: truly You are the Son of God. The King who provides bread and masters the sea is moving on toward the greater bread of His broken body and the greater rescue of the cross and resurrection.

quiz.q: How did Jesus respond when the disciples wanted to send the hungry crowds away?

options.text: He told the disciples to feed them, then fed thousands from five loaves and two fish.
options.note: Verses 16-21 record His command and the miraculous feeding.

options.text: He agreed and dismissed the crowds to buy food in the villages.
options.note: The opposite; He refused to send them away and fed them Himself.

options.text: He told the crowds to fast until morning.
options.note: He fed them at once rather than commanding a fast.

options.text: He sent the disciples to buy enough food for everyone.
options.note: They had only five loaves and two fish; Jesus multiplied them rather than sending the disciples to buy.

quiz.q: What was left over after the five thousand were fed?

options.text: Twelve basketfuls of broken pieces, showing the abundance of the provision.
options.note: Verse 20 records the twelve baskets left over after all were satisfied.

options.text: Just enough was used, with nothing to spare.
options.note: In fact there was abundant surplus, twelve baskets, underscoring the miracle.

options.text: A few scraps that were thrown away.
options.note: The leftovers were twelve full baskets deliberately gathered, not scraps discarded.

options.text: Nothing, because the food ran out.
options.note: All ate and were satisfied with twelve baskets left over; the food did not run out.

quiz.q: What did Jesus say as He came to the disciples on the water?

options.text: Take courage, It is I, do not be afraid, words carrying the resonance of the divine name.
options.note: Verse 27 gives His reassurance, echoing the I am of God's self-revelation.

options.text: Nothing; He simply climbed into the boat.
options.note: He spoke first to calm their terror before anything else.

options.text: A rebuke for their being out on the water at night.
options.note: He reassured them rather than rebuking them for the crossing He had commanded.

options.text: A command to row harder against the wind.
options.note: He calmed their fear and later stilled the wind; He did not tell them to row harder.

quiz.q: What happened when Peter walked toward Jesus on the water?

options.text: He walked while looking to Jesus, began to sink in fear, cried out, and was caught by Jesus' hand.
options.note: Verses 29-31 show his faith, his fear, and his rescue.

options.text: He walked the whole way without any difficulty.
options.note: He did walk, but began to sink when afraid; the rescue is central.

options.text: He refused to leave the boat at all.
options.note: He did step out and walk before beginning to sink.

options.text: He sank and drowned in the sea.
options.note: Jesus immediately reached out and took hold of him; he did not drown.

quiz.q: How did the disciples respond after Jesus stilled the wind?

options.text: They worshiped Him, saying, Truly You are the Son of God.
options.note: Verse 33 records their worship and confession.

options.text: They were relieved but said nothing about who He was.
options.note: They did more than feel relief; they worshiped and confessed Him the Son of God.

options.text: They argued about how He had done it.
options.note: Their response was worship and confession, not debate.

options.text: They asked Him to leave them as the Gadarenes had.
options.note: They worshiped Him rather than asking Him to depart.

questions: Jesus felt compassion for the crowds even in His own grief over John. How does His mercy in the midst of sorrow encourage you to bring your needs to Him?
The disciples saw only five loaves and two fish, but Jesus saw enough to feed thousands. Where do you see only your lack, and how might you offer what little you have to Him?
On the sea Jesus said, It is I, do not be afraid. What storm are you facing where you need to hear His voice and fix your eyes on Him?
Peter began to sink when he looked at the wind instead of Jesus, yet was caught the moment he cried out. When you falter, do you cry to Christ to save you, trusting He will hold you fast?
The disciples worshiped Jesus as the Son of God after seeing His power. How does a fresh sight of who Jesus is move you to worship Him this week?

translationNotes.note: The fourth watch of the night was between three and six in the morning, the darkest, most exhausting hours, when Jesus came to the struggling disciples.
It is I renders the Greek ego eimi, I am, which carries the resonance of the divine name revealed to Moses in Exodus 3:14.
Truly You are the Son of God is a high confession of worship, anticipating Peter's fuller confession in Matthew 16:16.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 14:13-36. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; line-break and note markers were skipped. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
